region_of_origin: Japan / China (Japanese yuzu citrus, Chinese-style jelly preparation; served in Singapore Chinese restaurant context)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific yuzu jelly entry exists in FoodOn. FOODON_03301710 (jelly food product) is the closest parent category. Yuzu-specific jelly is not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJX (Jelly dessert) is the closest parent. No yuzu-specific jelly code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for yuzu jelly. General food concepts in SNOMED CT do not extend to this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard individual serving of yuzu jelly is approximately 120 g, typically presented as a single chilled portion cup as served at Din Tai Fung and similar Chinese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fruit jelly dessert, commercially prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods; supplemented with yuzu juice composition data from USDA FoodData Central and peer-reviewed yuzu nutritional literature
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for yuzu jelly exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated by component modelling: a standard fruit jelly dessert base (USDA FDC NDB No. 19300, gelatin dessert prepared) adjusted for yuzu juice contribution (~10–15% by weight), honey (~5%), and dried yuzu peel (~2%). Yuzu juice nutritional profile sourced from USDA FDC entry for citrus juice (closest analogue) and peer-reviewed data (Tsurunaga et al., 2013, Journal of Nutritional Science and Vitaminology). Energy and macros reflect a lightly sweetened, predominantly water-and-sugar jelly matrix.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fruit jelly / gelatin-based desserts — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fruit jelly and honey-sweetened gelatin desserts
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for yuzu jelly. GI estimated at 46 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fruit jelly desserts (GI range 35–55) and honey (GI ~55). The dominant carbohydrate matrix is sucrose and fructose from honey and yuzu, set in a gelatin/agar base with high water content, which moderates glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (46 × 15.8 g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.2 serving factor) / 100 = approximately 8.7 for a 120 g serving (carbohydrate ~19.0 g per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~28 mg per 100 g; ~34 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Yuzu jelly contains minimal sodium (~28 mg per 100 g), making it a low-sodium dessert option that poses negligible concern for blood pressure management in hypertensive individuals when consumed as an occasional single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content (~13.5 g sugar per 100 g; ~16.2 g per serving) with low GI (~46)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although yuzu jelly has a low glycemic index (~46), its sugar content of approximately 16 g per 120 g serving warrants portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, and it should be counted as part of the daily carbohydrate allowance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and low sodium; yuzu provides flavonoids with potential cardioprotective properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Yuzu jelly is very low in saturated fat and sodium, and yuzu citrus contains flavonoids (notably naringenin and hesperidin) that have been associated with cardioprotective effects in peer-reviewed literature, making it a low cardiovascular concern dessert in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yuzu Jelly (柚子果冻) is a chilled, set-gelatin dessert featuring the distinctive aromatic citrus flavour of yuzu (Citrus junos), a Japanese citrus fruit prized for its floral, tart, and slightly bitter profile. As served at Din Tai Fung in Singapore, the dessert combines yuzu marmalade, honey, and dried yuzu peel within a lightly sweetened gelatin or agar matrix, producing a translucent, wobbling jelly with a refreshing sweet-sour finish. The texture is smooth and delicate, melting on the palate, with the yuzu peel providing occasional bursts of concentrated citrus aroma. Nutritionally, yuzu jelly is a low-energy dessert (~68 kcal per 100 g) dominated by simple sugars (~13.5 g per 100 g) from honey and yuzu marmalade, with negligible fat and modest protein from gelatin. Its low glycemic index (~46) reflects the fructose and sucrose matrix moderated by the high water content (~82 g per 100 g) and gelatin structure. Yuzu provides a meaningful contribution of vitamin C (~6.5 mg per 100 g) and flavonoids. Clinically, the dessert is low in sodium and saturated fat, making it suitable for hypertensive and cardiovascular-conscious individuals in moderate portions; however, its sugar content requires portion awareness for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets when plant-based agar is used as the setting agent.

Is yuzu jelly suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for yuzu jelly exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA) or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by component modelling from USDA FDC gelatin dessert and citrus juice entries, adjusted for yuzu-specific ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fruit jelly entries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.

The dish is classified as Chinese cuisine per the input and restaurant context (Din Tai Fung Singapore), but the primary flavouring ingredient (yuzu) is of Japanese origin; multilingual names therefore include both Chinese and Japanese forms. 18. Multilingual naming follows Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in a Singapore restaurant context, with Japanese names additionally included given the Japanese ingredient origin. 19. GI of 46 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement data for this specific product formulation is not available in any approved GI source.
